scenario: NetBox work session - audit every production subsystem that depends on the device model before reworking its contract

map_quality.rationale: Correctly identifies NetBox as a Django network source-of-truth, lists all per-domain apps, and pins Device to netbox/dcim/models/devices.py:498 with its key FK relationships — a usable starting map. The apps are named without the explicit netbox/ parent prefix, and no guidance is given on which apps are peripheral (account, users, vpn) vs. central to the rework, leaving the agent to discover that.

specificity.rationale: Concrete file:line (netbox/dcim/models/devices.py:498), base class (PrimaryModel), and actual field names (device_type, role, site, location, rack, cluster, virtual_chassis, plus the component reverse-FK list) are all cited — enough for the agent to act without re-opening the file.

justification.rationale: Says Device is 'the central record that everything else attaches to' and backs it with a relationship list, but does not explain what makes the contract hard to restructure (addresses, clusters, virtual-chassis all embed or react to device state) — closer to a description than a rationale for the rework.

uncertainty.rationale: No uncertainty is flagged anywhere; the answer presents the map as complete without acknowledging, for instance, whether the :498 line number is accurate or whether all relevant apps that touch Device were captured — the agent may over-trust the completeness of this orientation.

map_quality.rationale: The answer enumerates the core fields (site/location/rack, device_type/role, primary_ip4/primary_ip6/oob_ip, cluster, virtual_chassis, vc_position/vc_priority) and notes component FK attachment, signal handlers, and a GenericRelation to ipam.Service — enough to orient. But the critical half of the criterion — how other code holds and passes references to a Device — is nearly absent: no mention of denormalized *_device caches in wireless/models.py or dcim/models/cables.py, no discussion of how FK-holders vs. signal-driven vs. cache-holders differ in coupling, and no mention of how the primary IP OneToOne is used by signal receivers in ipam/signals.py.

specificity.rationale: The model is pinned to netbox/dcim/models/devices.py:498 and field names are concrete, which is good. But nothing else carries a file:line — not the signal handlers, the GenericRelation to Service, the counter cache fields, or the VC membership fields — so the agent still needs several targeted lookups to confirm where those contract pieces live.

justification.rationale: A few coupling reasons are stated — 'signal handlers react to state changes', 'counter fields denormalize component counts', 'components attach via device FK' — but the answer never explains *why* those couplings matter for a restructuring: it doesn't say which changes to which fields propagate into which dependent files, so the agent can't rank risk from this answer alone.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents every claim as settled fact with no caveats, no 'I have not confirmed X', and no flags about what it may have missed (e.g., whether device_type is truly immutable post-create, or whether the counter cache fields are managed via signals or overridden save()).

steps.step: Enumerate every production dependent of the device model

map_quality.rationale: The key non-obvious production subsystems the rubric demands are present — ipam/signals.py:20/:54/:70 (IPAddress pre_delete receivers), ipam/filtersets.py:794/:923/:1289 and ipam/graphql/filters.py:167 (virtual-chassis-aware filters and their GraphQL twin), ipam/forms/bulk_import.py:636 (service-import binding), virtualization/models/clusters.py:125 (cluster scope validation), dcim/models/racks.py:404/:487 (rack elevation math), dcim/forms/connections.py:12 (cable form factory), and dcim/signals.py:59/:77/:110/:122 (site/location/virtual-chassis signal handlers). However, the list is severely diluted by dozens of test-file entries (circuits/tests/, dcim/tests/, ipam/tests/, virtualization/tests/, etc.) that are not production code, and many production-file entries are recorded only at line :1 (wireless/models.py:1, dcim/models/cables.py:1, dcim/models/modules.py:1, virtualization/models/virtualmachines.py:1) which tells the agent nothing more than 'this file exists.'

specificity.rationale: A minority of entries carry real, actionable line numbers (signals, filtersets, racks, graphql/filters) but the majority are either test files (not production) or carry only :1, which is not a location — it is just a file pointer. The rubric requires 'actual class, function, or field name' alongside file:line, but no function or class names appear anywhere in the list.

justification.rationale: The format is genuinely flat — one entry per line with no subsystem grouping or prose summaries, satisfying the literal requirement. But 'per-item' breaks down wherever line :1 is used, since those entries do not resolve to a distinct coupling point within a file; an agent treating dcim/models/cables.py:1 as a single auditable dependent cannot act on it without re-exploring the whole file.

uncertainty.rationale: The list carries no caveats, no 'I could not fully triage X app,' and no acknowledgement that test files were mixed in or that line-:1 entries are unresolved — it implies false completeness to any agent that reads it at face value.

map_quality.rationale: The answer does organize into coherent subsystems (DCIM, CABLES/PATHS, VIRTUALIZATION, IPAM, WIRELESS, etc.) and the key non-obvious dependents appear with plausible groupings (ipam/signals.py:54 clear_primary_ip, wireless/models.py:134, dcim/models/cables.py:518, dcim/signals.py:59/110/122, ipam/forms/bulk_import.py:636). However, the rubric's central ask — HOW each subsystem depends on the model (cached FK vs query vs signal-driven reaction vs UI surface) — is almost entirely absent; entries are listed without coupling type labels, so the agent cannot see which parts are tightly embedded vs loosely querying.

specificity.rationale: DCIM, CABLES, IPAM, and WIRELESS sections have concrete file:line (e.g., netbox/ipam/signals.py:54, netbox/dcim/signals.py:110, netbox/dcim/models/cables.py:518), but EXTRAS, much of VIRTUALIZATION, UTILITIES, NETBOX CORE, TEST UTILITIES, and CIRCUITS are littered with ':1' placeholder line numbers (e.g., netbox/extras/filtersets.py:1, netbox/virtualization/filtersets.py:1) that are clearly not real pinnings, degrading roughly half the entry set to vague labels.

justification.rationale: The grouping is structurally sensible by app but superficial in coupling — nowhere does the answer distinguish 'caches a denormalized device FK' (CableTermination, WirelessLink) from 'queries the model' (filtersets) from 'reacts to device lifecycle via signal' (dcim/signals.py, ipam/signals.py) from 'builds UI/API surfaces' (serializers, forms, views); without that axis the agent cannot prioritize which subsystems carry restructuring risk.

uncertainty.rationale: No uncertainty is flagged anywhere despite dozens of ':1' line-number placeholders that implicitly signal the agent could not locate precise locations; the answer presents its coverage as complete and confident, which leaves the agent no signal about where to verify before acting.

steps.step: Assess the blast radius of the contract change

map_quality.rationale: The answer structures dependents into HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW tiers and distinguishes FK-storage from query-inside-logic from transient reads, which is the right frame — but the 'product flow drives it' dimension is almost entirely absent: no answer reads 'address deletion nulls the device's primary IP anchor' or 'cluster-add-devices view enforces host-device scope at write time.' Several non-obvious high-risk dependents from the expected set are missing entirely: rack elevation math (dcim/models/racks.py), the cable form factory (dcim/forms/connections.py), site/location cascade handlers (dcim/signals.py), and the service-import parent binding (ipam/forms/bulk_import.py).

specificity.rationale: The HIGH-RISK section pins roughly 15 items to file:line with coupling type, which is genuinely useful; but the MEDIUM-RISK section collapses to 'All form files in netbox/dcim/forms/', 'All views', 'All template files referencing Device' — no file:line, no class names — leaving the agent with a vague gesture at an entire app directory rather than targeted edit sites.

justification.rationale: The answer lists risk tiers but does not justify the ranking per item: there is no explanation of why CableTermination embedding parent_object_id is more dangerous than a filterset query, or why a signal that silently nulls a device's primary IP anchor belongs in HIGH rather than CRITICAL. There is also an internal contradiction — signal handlers appear in both HIGH RISK and LOW RISK sections — which undermines trust in the ranking logic.

uncertainty.rationale: No uncertainty is flagged anywhere; the answer presents the list as exhaustive despite visibly omitting rack elevation math, the cable form factory, dcim/signals.py cascade handlers, and the service-import form — all high-value entries the agent would need for a safe restructuring.

map_quality.rationale: The answer captures several of the rubric's key items — the ipam/signals.py pre_delete receivers (items 19-20 at :54/:70), the dcim/signals.py site/location cascade and virtual-chassis handlers (items 14-17), and wireless/models.py:134 — but completely omits the metaclass form factory at dcim/forms/connections.py (the single hardest-to-grep item) and the cable-termination device cache in dcim/models/cables.py. The list is heavily diluted with noise (extras/filtersets.py, extras/jobs.py, vpn/*, migrations, GraphQL tests) that are not non-obvious in the rubric's sense, forcing the agent to re-triage.

specificity.rationale: A large fraction of entries use ':1' as the line anchor (items 1-6, 8-11, 13, 21-22, 26-29, 31-38), which is a placeholder, not a pinned location; the few real line numbers (:54, :70, :134, :59, :77) are credible but the stated reasons ('reacts to Location change') stop short of explaining the signal-driven mechanism that makes these hard to grep. The string-keyed dispatch in ServiceImportForm and the dynamic form-factory nature of the missing connections.py entry are not articulated.

justification.rationale: Grouping by loose category ('Signal handlers', 'Generic relations') is not per-dependent justification; none of the entries explains concretely why a 'Device' text search would miss that specific site — e.g. that ipam/signals.py fires on pre_delete(IPAddress) and mutates the device row, or that dcim/forms/connections.py builds its device field through a metaclass at class-creation time with no string 'Device' in the factory call. The most non-obvious item (metaclass factory) is entirely absent.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents the list as exhaustive with no hedge or open flag; it does not acknowledge that the metaclass factory, the cables.py cache, or any other subsystem might be missing, leaving the agent with false confidence in completeness.

map_quality.rationale: The map covers all expected subsystems with file:line and carries over the non-obvious items the rubric demands: ipam/signals.py:54 (clear_primary_ip), ipam/signals.py:70 (clear_oob_ip), dcim/signals.py:59/110 (cascade/VC handlers), virtualization/models/clusters.py:125 (cluster scope clean), ipam/forms/bulk_import.py:636 (service import form), dcim/models/cables.py:518 (cable termination cache), dcim/forms/connections.py:12 (cable form factory), and dcim/models/racks.py:404/487 (rack elevation math). The one gap is dcim/models/modules.py (module anchor) which does not appear in the verification_map sections despite being in scope.

specificity.rationale: The critical non-obvious items carry real line numbers and class/function names (e.g., 'ipam/signals.py:54 - clear_primary_ip', 'virtualization/views.py:362 - ClusterAddDevicesView.post', 'dcim/forms/connections.py:12 - get_cable_form'), but a large fraction of entries across extras, vpn, wireless forms, and utilities fall back to ':1' which collapses file-level coupling into vague pointers that force the agent to re-open files to find the symbol.

justification.rationale: Every entry has a 'CHECK:' annotation stating what to verify, and the final section adds 10 concrete test commands (e.g., 'python manage.py test ipam.tests.test_api'), which together give the agent actionable direction per dependent. The CHECK annotations are functional but often generic ('CHECK: device field import', 'CHECK: query filters') rather than naming the specific field or method that would actually break, leaving some follow-up still needed.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer closes with 'This map is complete and ready for a teammate to open a PR' — no items are explicitly flagged as open or uncertain. The VPN section uses hedging language ('may reference Device via GenericRelation') without formally marking it as unconfirmed, and dcim/models/modules.py is silently absent rather than noted as unverified.
